evidence: All 10 toolkit tools follow a uniform signature pattern: (session: DemosSession, opts: T) => Promise<ToolResult<R>>, with connect/disconnect as documented exceptions using throw/void semantics
Every tool uses withToolWrapper for consistent timing, error wrapping, and observer notification — no tool bypasses this pattern
All tools validate input with validateInput(Schema, opts) as their first operation, returning err() on failure
Consistent error contract: all tools return typed ToolResult<T> with DemosError codes, never throwing (except connect which documents its exception)
Guards use a consistent check-then-record pattern with boolean parameter (record: true/false) for atomic operations
The barrel export in toolkit/index.ts cleanly separates types, tools, guards, and schemas into labeled sections
No public functions exceed 5 parameters — tools take (session, opts), guards take (store, walletAddress, ...domain-specific)
api_surface_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: connect() uses throw semantics while all other tools return ToolResult — documented but still a shape inconsistency that consumers must handle differently. scan() has an optional opts parameter while most other tools require it.

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The toolkit API surface is highly coherent. All tools share the same (session, opts) => ToolResult<T> signature, wrapped uniformly by withToolWrapper, with Zod schema validation as the entry gate. The connect/disconnect exception is well-documented and justified by its lifecycle-management role. Guards follow their own consistent internal pattern with check-and-record semantics.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: A developer seeing one tool can accurately predict the shape of all others — this meets the 90-level anchor. The connect/disconnect throw-vs-result divergence and minor opts optionality inconsistency prevent a higher score, but these are documented design decisions rather than accidental drift. Score reflects strong coherence with minor documented exceptions.

issues.summary: scan() takes optional opts while most sibling tools require their options parameter
evidence: scan(session, opts?) vs tip(session, opts), react(session, opts), attest(session, opts) — all siblings require their options object
issues.suggestion: Make scan's opts required with sensible defaults inside the ScanOptions type, or document the intentional divergence in the function docstring explaining why a no-args scan is valid

possible_false_positives: scan_opts_optional may be intentional since scanning with defaults is a valid use case

add.description: All 10 toolkit tools share (session, opts) => ToolResult<T> with withToolWrapper providing consistent timing, error wrapping, and observer hooks. This makes the API surface predictable for consumers.

add.header: Zod validation as universal entry gate
add.description: Every tool calls validateInput(Schema, opts) as its first operation, giving consumers consistent early-fail behavior with structured error messages across all tools.

add.header: Connect uses throw semantics by design
add.description: connect/disconnect throw DemosError rather than returning ToolResult because they manage session lifecycle, not tool operations. This is documented in the connect.ts header comment and the barrel export separation.
